text:
COMPREHENSIVE SOLID TUMOR NEXT GENERATION SEQUENCING REPORT

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Patient Name: Gerald T. Holloway
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Male
Medical Record Number: [on file]
Ordering Clinician: Medical Oncology / Gastroenterology
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) colonoscopic biopsy
Anatomic Site: Sigmoid colon mass
Procedure: Colonoscopic biopsy
Submitting Institution: [on file]
Tumor Content (estimated): 55% (reviewed and confirmed by board-certified pathologist prior to nucleic acid extraction)
Nucleic Acid Quality: DNA concentration 42 ng/uL; DIN 4.2 (acceptable for FFPE); RNA quality acceptable
Panel: Comprehensive Solid Tumor Panel (500+ gene; DNA + RNA-based, including SNV, indel, CNV, fusion, and TMB/MSI analysis)
Sequencing Platform: Illumina NovaSeq 6000
Mean Coverage Depth: 750x (DNA); 1,200x (hotspot regions)
Report Version: Final

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CLINICAL INDICATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Stage IVA (AJCC 8th edition) colorectal adenocarcinoma, sigmoid colon, with synchronous hepatic metastases (4 lesions). Prior hotspot sequencing on biopsy specimen identified KRAS p.G12D mutation. MSI status by IHC: microsatellite stable (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+). Comprehensive genomic profiling requested for treatment planning, clinical trial eligibility, and identification of additional actionable alterations.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUMMARY OF FINDINGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This specimen harbors KRAS p.G12D as the primary oncogenic driver. The tumor is microsatellite stable (MSS) by NGS and demonstrates low tumor mutational burden (TMB-L). No high-tier actionable alterations eligible for approved targeted therapies are identified at this time. KRAS G12D renders this tumor ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab). MSS status and low TMB render this tumor unlikely to respond to immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy.

TIER I/II ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS (per AMP/ASCO/CAP guidelines):
- KRAS p.G12D -- Tier IA: loss of response to anti-EGFR therapy (negative predictive biomarker); KRAS G12D-selective inhibitors under clinical investigation
- No Tier I therapeutic actionability for approved agents identified

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SOMATIC ALTERATIONS DETECTED
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

SNVs AND SMALL INDELS

1. KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A)
   Gene function: Proto-oncogene; RAS GTPase; central node of MAPK/ERK and PI3K signaling
   Variant type: Missense substitution
   Exon: 2
   Variant allele frequency (VAF): 41%
   Coverage at locus: 812x
   Classification: Pathogenic / Oncogenic Driver (Tier IA)
   Notes: KRAS codon 12 mutations are among the most common oncogenic alterations in colorectal adenocarcinoma, occurring in approximately 40% of cases. The G12D substitution (Gly→Asp) impairs intrinsic GTPase activity of the KRAS protein, resulting in constitutive activation of RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling cascades. This specific mutation is present in approximately 12-13% of all colorectal cancers and approximately 30% of KRAS-mutant CRCs. KRAS G12D is a negative predictive biomarker for response to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies (cetuximab and panitumumab); such therapy is contraindicated. KRAS G12D-selective small molecule inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133 and related compounds) are currently under evaluation in Phase I/II clinical trials, and this mutation may confer eligibility for such trials.

2. APC p.R1450* (c.4348C>T)
   Gene function: Tumor suppressor; Wnt/beta-catenin pathway regulator
   Variant type: Nonsense (stop-gain)
   Exon: 16 (mutation cluster region)
   VAF: 44%
   Coverage at locus: 788x
   Classification: Pathogenic / Loss-of-function (Tier IB)
   Notes: APC truncating mutations are the earliest and most common somatic events in sporadic colorectal tumorigenesis, identified in over 80% of colorectal adenocarcinomas. The p.R1450* variant results in premature truncation of the APC protein, abolishing its ability to form the beta-catenin destruction complex and resulting in constitutive nuclear beta-catenin signaling. The near-equal VAF to that of KRAS suggests this represents a co-occurring founder/clonal event, consistent with the classical adenoma-to-carcinoma sequence in sporadic CRC. No germline APC variant is reported; absence of polyposis phenotype (no synchronous adenomatous polyps on colonoscopy) is consistent with somatic, sporadic acquisition.

3. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A)
   Gene function: Tumor suppressor; transcription factor; genomic guardian
   Variant type: Missense substitution (gain-of-function hotspot)
   Exon: 5
   VAF: 39%
   Coverage at locus: 804x
   Classification: Pathogenic / Gain-of-function oncogenic (Tier IB)
   Notes: TP53 p.R175H is one of the most well-characterized gain-of-function (GOF) hotspot mutations in human cancer. Unlike simple loss-of-function TP53 mutations, the R175H variant not only abrogates wild-type p53 tumor suppressor function but also endows the mutant protein with neomorphic oncogenic activities, including promotion of invasion, resistance to apoptosis, and dysregulation of gene expression programs. This mutation is detected in approximately 10-15% of colorectal cancers. The VAF of 39% is consistent with clonal acquisition. TP53 alterations in CRC generally indicate poor prognosis and are associated with chromosomal instability (CIN) phenotype, consistent with the copy number alterations detected in this specimen.

NO ADDITIONAL PATHOGENIC SNVs/INDELS DETECTED IN CLINICALLY RELEVANT GENES:
- NRAS: Wild-type (exons 2, 3, 4 fully sequenced; no codons 12, 13, 59, 61, 117, 146 mutations)
- HRAS: Wild-type
- BRAF: Wild-type (V600E and non-V600 hotspots negative; V600 codon directly confirmed WT)
- PIK3CA: Wild-type (exons 9, 20; H1047R/L and E542K/E545K not detected)
- PTEN: Wild-type (no pathogenic SNV or indel)
- SMAD4: Wild-type (no pathogenic alteration; note: SMAD4 loss would confer poor prognosis in CRC and relative resistance to anti-EGFR therapy even in RAS WT tumors)
- FBXW7: Wild-type
- POLE: Wild-type (no pathogenic proofreading domain mutation; consistent with MSS, low TMB)
- POLD1: Wild-type
- RNF43: Wild-type
- CTNNB1: Wild-type (no activating beta-catenin exon 3 mutations)
- ERBB2 (HER2): Wild-type (no activating mutation)
- MET: Wild-type (no exon 14 skip mutation or amplification)
- FGFR1/2/3/4: Wild-type (no activating mutation)
- IDH1/2: Wild-type
- BRCA1: Wild-type
- BRCA2: Wild-type
- ATM: Wild-type
- CDK12: Wild-type
- CHEK2: Wild-type (no pathogenic variant)
- MLH1: Wild-type (no somatic mutation; consistent with IHC retention)
- MSH2: Wild-type
- MSH6: Wild-type
- PMS2: Wild-type
- NF1: Wild-type (no loss-of-function)
- NF2: Wild-type
- STK11: Wild-type
- KEAP1: Wild-type
- ARID1A: Wild-type
- ARID2: Wild-type
- RB1: Wild-type

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS (CNAs)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. MYC Amplification (8q24.21)
   Copy number: ~6 (diploid baseline)
   Classification: Likely oncogenic; Tier IIB
   Notes: MYC amplification/gain at 8q24 is present in approximately 20-30% of colorectal cancers and is associated with more aggressive tumor biology, poor differentiation, and worse clinical outcomes. MYC overexpression downstream of APC/Wnt activation further amplifies proliferative signaling. Currently no approved MYC-directed therapy; no actionable implication for standard-of-care treatment selection.

2. CDK8 Gain (13q12)
   Copy number: ~4
   Classification: Variant of uncertain significance for direct actionability; potentially oncogenic in context
   Notes: CDK8 is a transcriptional kinase involved in Mediator complex function and Wnt pathway transcriptional output. CDK8 gain/amplification has been reported in approximately 30% of colorectal cancers and has been associated with poor prognosis. CDK8 inhibitors are under preclinical and early clinical investigation but are not currently part of standard management.

3. Chromosome 20q Gain (20q11.2-q13.33)
   Copy number: ~3-4 (broad arm-level gain)
   Classification: Recurrent CRC alteration; Tier IIB
   Notes: Chromosome 20q gain is one of the most frequently observed chromosomal copy number changes in colorectal cancer, identified in 30-65% of cases. The 20q amplicon commonly includes genes such as BCAS1, ZNF217, and AURKA. This finding, together with the APC mutation, TP53 p.R175H, MYC gain, and overall copy number landscape, is consistent with a chromosomal instability (CIN) pathway CRC -- the most common molecular subtype of sporadic colorectal cancer.

4. ERBB2 (HER2): Not amplified
   Copy number: 2.1 (within normal diploid range)
   Notes: HER2 amplification/overexpression occurs in approximately 2-3% of metastatic CRC and, when present, can be targeted with trastuzumab-based combinations (e.g., Herceptin/pertuzumab or tucatinib/trastuzumab). HER2 amplification is not detected in this specimen; HER2-targeted therapy is not indicated.

NO OTHER CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT AMPLIFICATIONS OR DELETIONS DETECTED:
- EGFR: Not amplified
- FGFR1/2/3: Not amplified
- MET: Not amplified
- CDK4/6: Not amplified
- MDM2: Not amplified
- CDKN2A/B (9p21): No homozygous deletion detected
- PTEN: No copy number loss detected (consistent with WT by SNV analysis)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GENE FUSION / STRUCTURAL REARRANGEMENT ANALYSIS (RNA-based)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
No actionable or pathogenic gene fusions detected.

Genes specifically evaluated for fusions:
- ALK: No fusion detected (EML4-ALK and other partners negative)
- RET: No fusion detected
- NTRK1: No fusion detected
- NTRK2: No fusion detected
- NTRK3: No fusion detected
- ROS1: No fusion detected
- FGFR2/3: No fusion detected
- NRG1: No fusion detected
- RSPO2/3: No fusion detected (note: RSPO2/3 fusions occur in approximately 5-10% of RAS/RAF WT CRCs and are associated with Wnt pathway activation; not detected here)
- VTI1A-TCF7L2: Not detected

Notes: NTRK fusions are rare but actionable in colorectal cancer (larotrectinib, entrectinib); confirmed absent in this specimen. RET fusions occur in approximately 0.2% of CRCs; absent here.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TMB: 6 mutations/Megabase (mut/Mb)
Interpretation: TMB-Low (TMB-L)
Threshold applied: TMB-High defined as >=10 mut/Mb per FDA-approved pembrolizumab label

Notes: A low TMB in this MSS colorectal cancer is expected and consistent with the chromosomal instability (CIN) molecular pathway. Ultra-high TMB (>30-50 mut/Mb) can occasionally be seen in POLE/POLD1-mutant CRCs even in the absence of MSI; POLE and POLD1 are wild-type in this specimen. The low TMB and MSS phenotype together indicate that immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab/Keytruda, nivolumab) is not expected to be efficacious, consistent with the lack of benefit of anti-PD-1 therapy in MSS metastatic CRC demonstrated in multiple clinical trials.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) STATUS BY NGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MSI Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
Method: Analysis of >1,000 microsatellite loci genome-wide
MSI score: 2.1% (MSS threshold: <3.0%)

Notes: MSS status confirmed by both IHC (all four MMR proteins retained by immunohistochemistry: MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) and orthogonal NGS-based MSI analysis. This result excludes Lynch syndrome-associated MSI-H/dMMR CRC; MSI-H tumors would be eligible for pembrolizumab (Keytruda) as first-line therapy per FDA approval. No MLH1 promoter hypermethylation analysis requested on this specimen (clinically moot given confirmed retention of MLH1 protein by IHC).

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GERMLINE CONSIDERATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This report reflects analysis of tumor-only (somatic) DNA. Germline status cannot be definitively determined from somatic tissue sequencing. However, the following observations are relevant:

- No pathogenic variants detected in established hereditary CRC genes (APC somatic only; MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM not mutated; MSS phenotype inconsistent with Lynch syndrome)
- The APC p.R1450* variant detected at VAF 44% is interpreted as a somatic founder mutation based on clinical context (no FAP phenotype, no synchronous adenomatous polyposis on colonoscopy, no family history of CRC or polyposis)
- BRCA1/2 wild-type; no actionable homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) alterations identified
- Genetic counseling referral and germline testing may be considered per institutional protocol if clinically indicated, though the overall molecular profile is consistent with sporadic CRC

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MOLECULAR SUBTYPE CLASSIFICATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Consensus Molecular Subtype (CMS): Most consistent with CMS2 (Canonical/Epithelial)
  - Features supporting CMS2: MSS, chromosomal instability (CIN), APC driver mutation, TP53 mutation, 20q gain, MYC amplification, absence of BRAF V600E, high WNT/MYC transcriptional activation
  - CMS2 accounts for approximately 37% of CRCs and is characterized by epithelial differentiation, CIN, and activation of WNT and MYC signaling
  - CMS1 (MSI/immune) excluded: MSS, low TMB
  - CMS3 (metabolic) less likely: KRAS G12D present, but no PIK3CA or metabolic gene enrichment
  - CMS4 (mesenchymal) less likely: no TGF-beta pathway activation markers, no SMAD4 loss

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
RAS PATHWAY COMPREHENSIVE ANALYSIS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
KRAS exons 2-4 (codons 12, 13, 59, 61, 117, 146): KRAS p.G12D DETECTED (VAF 41%); all other codons wild-type
NRAS exons 2-4 (codons 12, 13, 59, 61, 117, 146): Wild-type
HRAS exons 2-4: Wild-type
BRAF exon 11, 15 (V600E and non-V600 hotspots including G469A, L597, K601, D594): Wild-type
NF1: Wild-type

RAS/RAF pathway conclusion: RAS pathway activation is mediated exclusively through KRAS p.G12D. BRAF wild-type status (including V600E negativity) excludes eligibility for BRAF+MEK inhibitor combinations (e.g., encorafenib/binimetinib as used in BRAF V600E-mutant CRC). The extended RAS panel (all codons per ESMO/ASCO guidelines) confirms full KRAS and NRAS mutational status. The patient is NOT eligible for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab/panitumumab) based on KRAS exon 2 mutation.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CLINICAL ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

APPROVED THERAPIES WITH DIRECT BIOMARKER RELEVANCE:

  NEGATIVE PREDICTIVE BIOMARKERS (therapies NOT indicated):
  - Anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab/panitumumab): CONTRAINDICATED -- KRAS exon 2 mutation (p.G12D)
  - Immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab): NOT indicated -- MSS, TMB-Low
  - BRAF-targeted therapy (encorafenib + cetuximab or binimetinib): NOT indicated -- BRAF wild-type
  - NTRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib): NOT indicated -- no NTRK fusion detected
  - RET inhibitors: NOT indicated -- no RET fusion detected
  - HER2-targeted therapy (trastuzumab/Herceptin + pertuzumab; tucatinib + trastuzumab): NOT indicated -- HER2 not amplified

  STANDARD-OF-CARE REGIMENS FOR KRAS-MUTANT METASTATIC CRC:
  - FOLFOX or FOLFIRI-based chemotherapy backbone remains standard of care
  - Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF): Appropriate in combination with first-line chemotherapy; no molecular exclusion
  - Aflibercept, ramucirumab: Appropriate in second-line irinotecan-based combinations
  - TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil), regorafenib: Appropriate in later-line settings

INVESTIGATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES:
  - KRAS G12D-selective inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133, opnurasib, and related compounds): Phase I/II clinical trials actively enrolling patients with KRAS G12D-mutant solid tumors including CRC. This patient may be eligible for such trials.
  - Combination approaches targeting KRAS G12D in combination with SOS1, MEK, SHP2 inhibitors or with chemotherapy: Under investigation
  - Note: KRAS G12C inhibitors (sotorasib/Lumakras, adagrasib/Krazati) are NOT applicable to this patient (G12D variant, not G12C)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
QUALITY METRICS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Specimen adequacy: Adequate (>20% tumor nuclei; estimated 55% tumor content)
DNA yield: Sufficient (>50 ng total input)
Mapping rate: 98.7%
Uniformity of coverage (>0.2x mean): 97.2%
Single nucleotide variant sensitivity: >99% at VAF ≥5% at 500x coverage
Copy number sensitivity: Gains ≥1.5 fold-change, deletions ≥0.5 fold-change detectable
Fusion sensitivity: >95% for expressed fusions at ≥10 reads

Limitations: This assay is performed on FFPE tumor tissue and is designed to detect somatic alterations. Germline variants cannot be excluded without matched normal tissue. Low-level subclonal variants at VAF <5% may not be reliably detected. Structural rearrangements outside of the targeted fusion panel are not assessed. Epigenetic alterations (e.g., promoter methylation) are not evaluated by this assay.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PATHOLOGIST ATTESTATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Case reviewed and reported by a board-certified pathologist with subspecialty expertise in molecular oncologic pathology. The results reported are interpreted in the context of the provided clinical history and tumor morphology.

This report is intended for use by qualified healthcare professionals for clinical decision-making in the context of the individual patient. Results should be interpreted in conjunction with all available clinical, pathological, and radiographic information.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
END OF REPORT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
